food_subgroup: Mashed leafy greens with lentils and tempering

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Keerai Masiyal. Nearest parent is a cooked leafy vegetable with legume dish. A new concept for South Indian mashed spinach preparations is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Cooked spinach and lentil mash, South Indian style (closest parent)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Keerai Masiyal; A03SM used as closest parent for cooked spinach-based preparations.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for Keerai Masiyal or equivalent South Indian mashed spinach-lentil preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard side dish serving of Keerai Masiyal is approximately 150g, typically served alongside plain rice and rasam as part of a Tamil home meal.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — cooked spinach and toor/moong dal component data
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods component data for cooked spinach (keerai) and toor/moong dal, combined in proportions typical of Keerai Masiyal. Ghee tempering contribution included for fat and cholesterol values. All values represent the finished dish per 100g.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index Database — estimated from cooked spinach and lentil mixture; very high dietary fibre content
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Keerai Masiyal. GI of approximately 32 estimated from University of Sydney GI Database entries for cooked spinach (GI ~15) and cooked lentils/dal (GI ~29–38). The very high dietary fibre content (3.2g/100g) and protein content (4.5g/100g) support a low GI classification. Glycaemic load per 150g serving calculated as (32 × 11.2 × 1.5) / 100 = 5.4.

hypertension.key_factor: High potassium-to-sodium ratio from spinach and dal
hypertension.guidance: Keerai Masiyal provides approximately 480mg potassium and 278mg sodium per 150g serving, yielding a favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio that supports blood pressure regulation; individuals on potassium-restricted diets due to renal complications should monitor intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycaemic index (~32) and high dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 32 and a glycaemic load of only 5.4 per 150g serving, Keerai Masiyal produces a minimal postprandial glucose response; the high fibre (3.2g/100g), protein (4.5g/100g), and magnesium (38mg/100g) content further support insulin sensitivity and glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high folate, potassium, and dietary fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Keerai Masiyal is low in saturated fat (1.5g/100g) and cholesterol (5mg/100g), and rich in folate (88mcg DFE/100g), potassium (320mg/100g), and dietary fibre (3.2g/100g), all of which contribute to cardiovascular risk reduction; the small amount of ghee used in tempering is nutritionally negligible at typical serving sizes.

anaemia.key_factor: High non-haem iron and folate content from spinach
anaemia.guidance: Keerai Masiyal provides 3.5mg iron and 88mcg DFE folate per 100g, making it one of the most iron- and folate-dense South Indian side dishes; consuming with vitamin C-rich foods (e.g., rasam) enhances non-haem iron absorption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Keerai Masiyal is a traditional South Indian Tamil dish consisting of cooked, mashed spinach (keerai) combined with soft-cooked toor dal (split pigeon peas) or moong dal, forming a thick, smooth, dark-green mash. The dish is finished with a hot ghee tempering (tadka) of mustard seeds, dried red chilli, and garlic, which adds fragrance, heat, and depth to the mild, earthy flavour of the spinach-lentil base. The texture is dense and creamy, with a slightly grainy quality from the mashed dal. Keerai Masiyal is one of the most nutritionally dense side dishes in South Indian cuisine, exceptionally high in iron (3.5mg/100g), folate (88mcg DFE/100g), calcium (95mg/100g), vitamin A (285mcg RAE/100g), and dietary fibre (3.2g/100g). It has a very low glycaemic index of approximately 32 and a glycaemic load of only 5.4 per standard 150g serving, making it highly suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or metabolic syndrome. It is traditionally eaten as a side dish alongside plain rice and rasam as part of a Tamil home meal, and is commonly served at South Indian restaurants in Singapore, Malaysia, and Tamil Nadu. The dish is vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free.
common_user_queries: South Indian spinach curry iron content

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from ICMR-NIN component data for cooked spinach and toor/moong dal rather than a direct laboratory analysis of the finished Keerai Masiyal dish. The proportions used are representative of traditional Tamil preparation. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for spinach and lentils. Overall confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data (MUFA and PUFA breakdown) for this traditional spinach-lentil preparation are not separately reported in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods component tables as of the accessed date (2026-04-13); ICMR-NIN reports total fat only for these ingredients. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the finished Keerai Masiyal dish is not separately tabulated in ICMR-NIN component data as of the accessed date; moisture varies with cooking method and water added during preparation. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — ghee in small tempering quantities contributes negligible trans fat per ICMR-NIN data). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients other than trace ghee, which contains no B12). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no vitamin D-contributing ingredients present). The FoodEx2 code A03SM is used as the closest parent; no exact code exists for Keerai Masiyal in the FoodEx2 hierarchy. GI value of 32 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for cooked spinach and lentil/dal preparations. Hindi local name (कीरई मसियल) is a transliteration of the Tamil name as no distinct Hindi culinary term exists for this dish.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Keerai Masiyal', the canonical English name for this Tamil dish. Tamil (கீரை மசியல்), Chinese simplified and traditional, Malay, and Hindi local names are all present.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (88.0), protein_g (4.5), fat_g (3.5), and carbohydrate_g (11.2) are all present, numeric, and non-zero. Source is ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, an approved primary regional source.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (3.2), sodium_mg (185.0), saturated_fat_g (1.5), and sugar_g (1.0) are all present as numeric values and are plausible for a spinach-lentil dish.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (32), gi_category ('low'), glycemic_load_per_serving (5.4), and gi_source are all present. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source. Estimation basis is documented.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodEx2 code A03SM used as closest parent with honest gap note. FoodOn and SNOMED CT are null with documented gap notes. No codes are invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, explains preparation method, texture, taste, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients. Suitable for RAG and LLM training.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (low concern), Type 2 Diabetes (low concern, human_condition_name correctly set to 'Type 2 Diabetes'), and Cardiovascular Disease (low concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases_by_language is populated for English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and romanized. All strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. Comprehensive English alternate names, Tamil script, Chinese script, Malay, and Hindi transliteration are all present.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Added explicit documentation of all -1 sentinel fields in notes to address G3 numeric hygiene failure.

Iteration 3: Expanded notes field to individually document each -1 field (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g) with specific reasons for data unavailability and citation of ICMR-NIN as the consulted source that confirmed unavailability, fully satisfying G3 numeric hygiene requirements per the negative sentinel values rule.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Keerai Masiyal passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard. The G3 numeric hygiene failure from iteration 2 has been resolved by explicitly documenting each -1 sentinel value (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g) in the notes field with specific reasons for data unavailability and the source consulted. Nutrient data is sourced from ICMR-NIN (approved primary regional source for Indian dishes) with adaptation documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database with full estimation basis documented. All three mandatory health conditions are present with specific clinical guidance. Multilingual coverage includes Tamil script, Chinese simplified and traditional, Malay, and Hindi transliteration. All 0.0 sentinel values are also documented. Ontology is honest with no invented codes.
